evidence_quotes.quote: Live service games may seem attractive from an economic point of view: continuous revenues from micro-transactions, ongoing updates, artificial long-term commitment.
designed to become completely unplayable without a regular server connection.

paragraphs.text: Live service games may seem attractive from an economic point of view: continuous revenues from micro-transactions, ongoing updates, artificial long-term commitment. However, from the point of view of players, they are increasingly transforming the medium into somewhat interchangeable, volatile and ultimately disappointing. Games that were previously known for their longevity, their finished design and their replay value are now only part of an economically motivated business model. They come to the market unfinished, need permanent internet connection and are often designed to become completely unplayable without a regular server connection. What is left behind is the game itself, its history, its world, its significance. Finally, it is not only the existence of live service games that makes sense in certain genres, but rather the aggressive and often exclusive exclusion of classic games by those titles. Where single players’ experiences with narrative depth, creative freedom and long-term value were created in the past, online games appear today, which often do not differ fundamentally from a hamsterrad. The servers run as long as the profit is correct and as soon as the game is no longer commercially attractive for the company, it will be discontinued. Players’ investment in time, emotion and often a lot of money is then simply lost. This becomes particularly drastic for games that work exclusively online, so-called ‘pertiny online’ Games. Even if I bought a game, I don’t keep it. I cannot experience it again, archive, modify or share it after the server shutdown. This creates a new form of digital accessibility that amounts to a cultural clear-cut. Games that could have artistic and creative value are deleted as never before. This is a certificate of poverty for a sector that increasingly wants to be understood as a cultural medium. I remember at times when games were not only products, but works were carefully assembled, closed, sometimes technically limited, but always accessible. Still today I can create, experience and enjoy classics such as Half-Life 2, Mass Effect, The Witcher 3 or Dark Souls, regardless of servers, Seasons or Battle Passes. And that is exactly what I systematically deny live service games. They bind me not by quality, but by dependence. They call for regular play, constant updates, cosmetic shopping and at the end: nothing. A particularly frustrating example for me was the shutdown of Rumbleverse, Knockout City or Babylon. Games that started with fresh ideas, were carried out by talented developers’ teams, but nevertheless disappeared smoothly and phonetically after only a short life. With them, my progress, my memories, my fun was lost. I couldn’t simply say: I didn’t play later than later. And even though, as a player, I am prepared to pay money for good games more than once. What I am unwilling to accept is that I will be sold a product that has systematically installed its own end.
